metadata.title: Complete the Loop: Turn JSON into Something a Human Can Use
metadata.playlist: Make It Smart — Scale Your Madison Intelligence
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 4 brief — Scale Your Thing & Add Intelligence

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 3 of 7. This is where most projects lose twenty-five points. The assignment says 'produce real outputs.' It also says, explicitly: raw JSON is not an output. If understanding what the workflow produced requires opening n8n or parsing a JSON file, the output is not done. The test is simple: could a non-technical person open this and understand it? A PDF, a CSV of recommendations, a chart image, an email — those pass the test. A JSON object in n8n's output panel does not. Today you pick one real output format, build the node that produces it, and document the end-to-end flow with a screenshot proving the artifact exists. Let's go.

props.segment: Complete the Loop: Turn JSON into Something a Human Can Use
props.command: Pick one real output format and build the node that produces it. JSON ≠ output.
props.runningText: test: could a non-technical person open this and understand it?
output: Safe defaults: formatted PDF/HTML report · CSV of recommendations · chart image
With TEST account: email/Slack notification · posted content
Document: Input → Processing → Output → Proof (screenshot/file link)

beats.narration_text: The raw JSON penalty is twenty-five points off. That is the largest single deduction in the rubric. It exists because the assignment is testing whether you can complete a workflow — not just wire one up. A workflow that produces raw JSON has not completed the loop. The data went in, the AI processed it, and the output sits in a data structure that requires a developer to read. That is not a finished product. The test for whether your output passes is this: give it to someone who has never seen your workflow. Can they tell what the workflow produced and what to do with it? If the answer is yes, the output passes. If they would need to ask 'what is this,' the output does not pass. Apply this test before you build the output node — pick the format that a non-technical person can open without asking for help.

artifactLines: Raw JSON output = -25 points. Largest single deduction in the rubric.
Why: the loop is not complete until the output is usable without a developer.
Test: give the output to a non-technical person. Can they tell what it is and what to do with it?
Pass: PDF, CSV, chart image, email, Slack message — all openable without n8n.
Fail: JSON object in n8n output panel, raw API response, data structure requiring code to read.
props.sparkLine: Could a non-technical person open this? That's the test.

beats.narration_text: Three output formats that always pass the test without needing any external account. First: a formatted PDF or HTML report. n8n can write an HTML file with the AI's output rendered as a report — title, date, a section per record with the AI's classification and insights. That file opens in any browser and a non-technical person can read it immediately. Second: a CSV of recommendations. The AI's output fields — category, confidence, key themes — exported as a CSV alongside the original title, date, and source. A CSV opens in any spreadsheet. Third: a chart image. If the AI is doing classification or scoring, you can aggregate the results and export a chart as an image file — a PNG of a bar chart showing the distribution of sentiment across the dataset. This requires more code but is the strongest visual proof that the AI did something. Start with the HTML report — it is the fastest to build, the easiest to screenshot, and the clearest demonstration that the output is done.

props.artifactHeading: Three formats that always pass — no account needed
artifactLines: 1. HTML/PDF report: n8n writes an HTML file — title, date, per-record AI output. Opens in browser.
2. CSV of results: AI output fields + original record fields exported to a spreadsheet. Opens anywhere.
3. Chart image: aggregate AI classifications into a PNG bar/pie chart. Strongest visual proof.
Start with HTML report: fastest to build, easiest to screenshot, clearest completion signal.
Rule: pick the format a non-technical person can open without asking what it is.
props.sparkLine: HTML report first. It's the fastest to build and the easiest to prove.

beats.act: if using email or posting — test accounts only
beats.narration_text: If your assignment involves sending emails, posting to Slack, or creating social media content, use a test account — never a real one. A test email account is a free email address you create for this project and never use for anything else. A test Slack workspace is a free workspace created for this project with only your account in it. A test social media account — if the platform allows it — is an account that is clearly labeled as a test or development account and never visible to the public. The reasons are practical, not just policy. Sending bulk automated emails from a real account can get it flagged as spam. Posting automated content to a real social account can get it suspended. And for this assignment, posting to a real account before the assignment is graded means a real audience sees potentially unpolished AI output before you have verified its quality. File outputs — HTML, CSV, chart image — have none of these risks and are the recommended default. Use live posting only if the assignment's output explicitly requires it and you have a test account.

artifactLines: Test email: a free address created only for this project — never your real address.
Test Slack: a free workspace with only your account — never a shared team workspace.
Test social: clearly labeled dev/test account, never public-facing.
Why: bulk automated email from real account → spam flag. Auto-posting to real account → suspension.
Default: file outputs (HTML, CSV, chart) have none of these risks. Use live posting only if required.
props.sparkLine: Test account or file output. Never a real account. Never spam.

beats.narration_text: The Part 2A documentation requires a specific structure: Input, Processing, Output, and Proof. Input: describe the records that go into the output step — what fields each one has, how many records. Processing: describe what the AI did to produce the output — what the prompt was, what fields it produced, what the output schema looks like. Output: describe the artifact — file format, file name, where it was saved, what a reader sees when they open it. Proof: a screenshot or a file link showing the artifact exists. A screenshot of the HTML report open in a browser, or a link to the CSV in your project folder, is sufficient proof. Without the proof, the documentation says 'it creates an output' but does not show one. That is the 'it would create X' trap — worth zero points for the output. The output must exist and you must show it.

artifactLines: Input: record fields + count going into the output step.
Processing: the prompt used · AI output fields · output schema.
Output: file format · file name · file location · what reader sees when they open it.
Proof: screenshot of the artifact open, or a file path that exists on disk.
Without proof: 'it would create X' = 0 pts for the output. The artifact must exist.
props.sparkLine: Input. Processing. Output. Proof. No 'would create' — only 'created.'

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Complete the Loop. Raw JSON is not an output — twenty-five points off if the loop is not closed. The test: could a non-technical person open this and understand it? Safe defaults that always pass: an HTML or PDF report, a CSV of recommendations, a chart image. If you are using email or social posting, use a test account created only for this project — never a real one, never anything that could be flagged as spam or automated posting. Documentation structure: Input, Processing, Output, and Proof. The Proof is a screenshot or file link — without it, the output does not exist as far as the grader is concerned. Episode 4 builds ten to fifteen examples from the same workflow to prove reliability.

props.artifactTitle: Complete the Loop: Turn JSON into Something a Human Can Use
artifactLines: Raw JSON = -25 pts. Test: could a non-technical person open this without help?
Safe defaults: HTML/PDF report · CSV of results · chart image.
Live posting: test account only. Never a real account. Never spam.
Doc: Input → Processing → Output → Proof (screenshot or file link).
'It would create X' = 0 pts. The artifact must exist and you must show it.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude — fill in the brackets with your actual project before running it. The prompt reads: My n8n workflow produces this AI output for each record: [describe your AI node's output schema — field names and what they mean]. My target audience for the final output is: [describe who will use it — a hiring manager, a researcher, a student, a marketing team]. My A2 problem was: [one sentence]. Pick the best human-usable output format for this project from: formatted HTML report, CSV of results, chart image, email to a test account. For the format you pick: write the n8n node configuration I need — which node, which settings, what the output file looks like. Include the HTML template or CSV schema. That prompt produces a complete node specification for your output step. Build it. Run the workflow once on your clean A3 data. Open the output file. If a non-technical person would understand it without explanation, the loop is closed.

props.segment: Complete the Loop: Turn JSON into Something a Human Can Use
props.command: My AI output schema: [fields + meanings]. Target audience: [who uses it]. A2 problem: [one sentence]. Pick the best human-usable format from: HTML report · CSV of results · chart image · test email. For the format you pick: n8n node config + settings + HTML template or CSV schema.
props.runningText: open the output file after building — if a non-technical person understands it, the loop is closed…
